\section{Introduction}
DOE national laboratories maintain scientific software of critical importance, encoding decades of expertise in numerical methods, simulation algorithms, and HPC architectures across Argonne, Oak Ridge, and Sandia National Laboratories~\cite{perabytes}. These codebases must evolve continuously---porting to new GPU architectures, incorporating new numerical methods, adapting to hardware constraints---while preserving correctness and reproducibility~\cite{keahey2025report,pouchard2019computational}. The complexity of HPC scientific software now outpaces human reviewers' ability to verify every AI-assisted change, creating a \textbf{software trustworthiness gap} with direct accountability risk for DOE's scientific mission.

LLM-based agents are now deployed across the national laboratory system to suggest code optimizations~\cite{podduturi2025ai}, identify performance bottlenecks~\cite{polu2025ai}, and translate legacy codebases~\cite{claude,rummler2026sandians}. However, LLMs hallucinate---generating recommendations that conflate numerically distinct algorithms, suggest transformations that break correctness, or cite validation results that do not support the proposed change~\cite{Ji2023HallucinationSurvey}, corrupting production scientific software and invalidating research results. Agentic systems~\cite{ezzrhari2020scalable} and retrieval-augmented generation~\cite{miyashita2024llm} partially address this, but two structural deficits prevent AI from reliably accelerating HPC software development:

\textbf{Absence of persistent knowledge state.} AI systems treat each interaction independently, with no persistent representation of a codebase's development history, performance characteristics, or prior decisions. Without this, AI agents re-analyze validated transformations from scratch on every query---multiplying latency and eliminating the compounding knowledge benefit that drives scientific acceleration.

\textbf{Lack of traceable provenance.} When an AI system recommends a code transformation, developers need to trace that recommendation to specific benchmarks, numerical analyses, or validation results. Current systems surface relevant documentation but provide no structured reasoning connecting evidence to recommendations---making AI-assisted changes impossible to audit, reproduce, or build upon.

\problem{The lack of structured, persistent, and traceable knowledge about HPC software development prevents AI agents from accelerating scientific software evolution and from providing recommendations that developers can trust and verify.}

To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{provenance-aware agentic knowledge graphs} (PA-AKG): foundational infrastructure for trustworthy, AI-accelerated HPC software engineering. PA-AKG gives AI agents a persistent, structured, and auditable representation of a codebase's current knowledge state. Knowledge graphs (KGs) represent complex, heterogeneous information through explicit semantic relationships~\cite{karras2024organizing,kousha2023democratizing}. PA-AKG encodes HPC software knowledge---algorithms, numerical methods, performance benchmarks, hardware constraints, validation results, and development decisions---as nodes linked by directed semantic edges. We extend prior KG work with agentic orchestration: as AI agents propose code transformations, they compare against KG-encoded performance expectations; confirmed decisions are written back with explicit provenance chains carrying the specific benchmarks and evidence that justify each change, accumulating an auditable development history that future agents build upon directly.

% [RF-1] "focus is tighter than the focus area — focusing on building software specifically rather than data moving through the models" — Reviewer 1 — verified by: AC-1
Beyond individual recommendations, PA-AKG is the \emph{knowledge-layer infrastructure} that curates and structures the signals AI systems must learn from in HPC contexts: compiler traces, performance profiles, benchmark logs, validation outcomes, and the git history connecting them. \textbf{Provenance is what transforms raw HPC data into trustworthy AI signal}: by linking every recommendation to the evidence that justifies it, PA-AKG eliminates redundant re-analysis and enables AI agents to accelerate code evolution by building on validated prior work---reducing the lag between research findings and deployed code optimizations. Within Focus Area 18, PA-AKG provides the provenance and knowledge layer that makes AI-assisted code tools---translation, curation, optimization---trustworthy by design: every AI-driven change is traceable to the evidence that warrants it, directly addressing the 18E call to learn from decades of DOE codes, compiler traces, and performance data in a trustworthy and reproducible manner~\cite{Ji2023HallucinationSurvey}. \vspace{-5pt}

\section{Project Objectives} 

Our goal is to design and implement a provenance-first agentic KG architecture with three integrated layers: knowledge representation; automation and extraction; and agentic orchestration (see Fig~\ref{fig:pa-akg}). PA-AKG is designed as a deployable knowledge-layer component in DOE's AI-assisted R\&D workflow: positioned between raw HPC artifacts (compiler traces, benchmark logs, git history) and AI agent reasoning, it provides the structured provenance record that makes AI-accelerated development trustworthy and scalable across the Genesis Mission portfolio. We will implement PA-AKG with role-specific agents (\eg ranking, evaluation, and synthesis agents), beginning in the software engineering research domain in the HPC context~\cite{cusati2026fose}, with secondary validation against HPC performance analysis workflows at Sandia National Laboratories. We will benchmark PA-AKG against retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), GraphRAG~\cite{Edge2024GraphRAG}, and static baselines on provenance completeness, reproducibility, and trustworthiness of AI-generated software decisions, and conduct controlled user studies with HPC software developers. Key risks include extraction reliability at HPC code scale and KG schema portability across domains; mitigated by the staged evaluation design and Sandia's HPC validation corpora. \textit{This project does not involve the construction, alteration, maintenance, and/or repair of public infrastructure in the United States.}

\section{Proposed Research and Methods}
\label{sec:methods}

Our research plan involves four main phases:

\paragraph{Phase 1 (Months 1--3): Knowledge Representation Layer and Initial Calibration.}
We will design the HPC software KG schema with entity types such as \textsc{Algorithm}, \textsc{NumericalMethod}, \textsc{CodeComponent}, \textsc{HardwareConstraint}, \textsc{Benchmark}, and \textsc{ValidationResult}---linked by semantic relations (\textsc{implements}, \textsc{requires}, \textsc{supersedes}, \textsc{validates}). A hybrid symbolic--semantic design pairs a property graph store with a vector index, supporting both structured relational queries and approximate similarity search. We will populate an initial KG from the software engineering research corpus established in prior work and validate entity extraction against manually annotated ground truth.
% [RF-5a-b] Phase 1 small-seed calibration study — verified by: AC-5b
\textbf{Small-seed calibration study.} Concurrently in Phase 1, we will run an initial calibration of the PA-AKG provenance pipeline against a small seed corpus of approximately 5--10 software engineering papers, beginning with Graph-Based RAG~\cite{Edge2024GraphRAG} as the canonical seed and snowballing backward through its references. The study proceeds in two modes: a \textit{discovery} mode in which the team hand-annotates claim--evidence pairs to establish baseline thresholds for provenance completeness and span-level alignment; and a \textit{calibration} mode in which those thresholds are refined to the empirical cutoff that distinguishes trustworthy from untrustworthy recommendations. Annotation is performed by team consensus (Brown, Wang, Cusati), cross-checked across members rather than relying on a single annotator. The output is a calibrated initial threshold set that feeds the Decision Gate Metrics at the Month-6 gate. The calibration depends on a populated KG, so the ordering inside Phase 1 is serial: schema $\rightarrow$ initial population $\rightarrow$ seed-corpus build $\rightarrow$ discovery $\rightarrow$ calibration.

\paragraph{Phase 2 (Months 4--6): Automation, Orchestration, and Interim Evaluation.}
% [RF-4] "Phase II proposal will be due before the evaluation phase is completed" — Reviewer 2 — verified by: AC-4
Phase 2 restructures the pre-proposal's evaluation plan to front-load interim benchmark results by Month 6, so that the Decision Gate and any Phase II Genesis submission can be informed by concrete evidence rather than projected outcomes. We will implement the extraction and automation pipeline: structured prompting will produce claim--evidence pairs; span-level alignment will link each claim to character-level source passage spans; and canonicalization auditing will normalize, validate, and deduplicate entity references to canonical KG nodes. The agentic orchestration layer, adapting the Planning \& Control pattern~\cite{Park2023GenerativeAgents}, introduces four role-specific agent types. \textit{Ranking agents} score candidate code changes by evidence strength and risk. \textit{Continuation agents} propose follow-on validation analyses grounded in KG gaps. \textit{Evaluation agents} execute reproducible benchmark workflows and write results back to the KG as structured evidence nodes. \textit{Synthesis agents} generate recommendation artifacts with full provenance metadata. Provenance inheritance is enforced at every agent handoff. PA-AKG explicitly separates \emph{traceability} (where a recommendation originates) from \emph{correctness} (whether it preserves numerical validity): evaluation agents verify each proposed transformation against KG-encoded benchmarks and flag discrepancies between literature claims and empirical results, preventing unverified provenance from propagating into the KG. Together, these agents form a closed autonomous loop: ranking and evaluation agents produce validated evidence that synthesis agents deliver to developers, while continuation agents identify KG gaps and drive further analysis---progressively reducing the re-analysis overhead that slows HPC software evolution.
% [RF-5a-c] Phase 2 expanded-corpus second calibration study — verified by: AC-5c
\textbf{Expanded-corpus second calibration and preliminary benchmark results.} By mid-Phase 2 we will expand the Phase 1 seed corpus via full snowball sampling to approximately 50 or more software engineering papers and re-run the calibration protocol against the larger corpus. The comparison of Phase 1 (small-corpus) thresholds against Phase 2 (expanded-corpus) thresholds provides the \emph{direct empirical evidence} of the scaling-behavior AI advantage claimed in the Decision Gate Metrics section (\S\ref{sec:decision-gate}). These preliminary benchmark results land at the \textbf{Month-6 Decision Gate}, providing the evidence base for the go/no-go decision and aligning with Phase II Genesis proposal preparation.

\paragraph{Phase 3 (Months 7--9): Full Evaluation and Generalization.}
% [RF-4] Phase 3 is labeled Full Evaluation, not the only evaluation phase — verified by: AC-4
With interim benchmark results delivered at Month 6, Phase 3 conducts the full evaluation of PA-AKG. We will complete the benchmark against vector-based RAG, hybrid retrieval, and GraphRAG-style reasoning~\cite{Edge2024GraphRAG} on the human-annotated claim--evidence benchmark from Phase 1--2. We will calculate span-level alignment precision/recall, Cohen's $\kappa$ with expert-labeled canonical entity clusters, and traceability completeness. A secondary HPC validation at Sandia National Laboratories assesses cross-domain generalizability of the extraction pipeline. Our second evaluation thread assesses provenance completeness and trustworthiness of AI-assisted code recommendations through controlled user studies with HPC software practitioners, collecting quantitative task-completion metrics and qualitative feedback on recommendation auditability.

\paragraph{Phase 4: Dissemination and Genesis Integration.}
We will release PA-AKG as an open-source package with a documented REST API, enabling other Genesis teams and DOE laboratory developers to integrate PA-AKG into their AI-assisted R\&D workflows without rebuilding the knowledge infrastructure. The API exposes provenance-aware KG queries so that translation agents, optimization agents, and other Genesis-funded tools can ground their recommendations in validated HPC knowledge. Generalization follows a staged pathway: the Sandia deployment (Kokkos, Trilinos, LAMMPS) establishes the PA-AKG schema and extraction pipeline for production HPC codebases; the open-source API release extends this to other Genesis projects, DOE laboratories, and the broader academic community. Academic dissemination targets ICSE, MSR, and SC to reach both software engineering researchers and HPC practitioners. We will present at Sandia and partner institutions to gather feedback and assess adoption pathways in active DOE workflows.

\section{Milestones}

\input{proposal_18E/milestones}

The expected outcomes include:
\begin{itemize}
    \item A design for provenance-aware knowledge graph architecture for trustworthy AI-assisted HPC scientific software development.
    \item An open-source PA-AKG implementation, consisting of an automated extraction pipeline and agentic orchestration, applied to HPC software development contexts.
    \item Benchmarking infrastructure to assess LLM-based systems for trustworthy code assistance in scientific software.
    \item Empirical insights on the provenance completeness, reproducibility, and trustworthiness of AI-assisted HPC code decisions.
    \item Broader scientific impact through open engagement with national laboratory and academic research communities, informing the design of next-generation trustworthy AI tools for HPC scientific software and establishing PA-AKG as a reference model for accountable AI in DOE's software mission.
\end{itemize}

% \paragraph{Research Team.} This project aligns with the prior work and trajectory of the research team, and the team has an established history of collaboration.
% [RF-3] "It is not clear from the proposal whether the team has worked together before" — Reviewer 1 — verified by: AC-3
% \textbf{Prior collaborations.} PI Brown and GRA Cusati have co-authored two prior works directly informing PA-AKG: an empirical study of how generative AI tools propagate evidence-based beliefs in software engineering~\cite{brown2025esem} and an ICSE-FoSE position paper articulating the need for structured knowledge accumulation in SE research~\cite{cusati2026fose}. The Brown--Wang VT partnership is a new cross-area collaboration within the Department of Computer Science at Virginia Tech, coordinated through weekly joint research meetings and shared data access within the PA-AKG project workspace. The VT--Sandia partnership with Co-PI Steinmetz is structured around quarterly on-site working sessions at Sandia, co-supervision of the Sandia-side validation corpus by Steinmetz and Cusati, and a shared data agreement governing access to Sandia HPC benchmarks and legacy code artifacts. \textbf{PI Brown} and GRA Cusati explored how LLMs propagate research-based evidence~\cite{brown2025esem} and outlined researchers' desire for structured knowledge accumulation~\cite{cusati2026fose} in software engineering contexts. For this work, Dr. Brown will lead the overall execution of the project---including the PA-AKG implementation and research plan. \textbf{Co-PI Wang} investigates LLMs in the context of science and society, with substantial experience studying the applications of LLMs (\ie~\cite{bi2024ai,srivastava2025llmthinkbench}) and agents (\ie~\cite{latimer2025hindsight,lu2024triageagent}) in scientific settings and earning an NSF CAREER award to extract patterns from research data~\cite{wang_career}. Dr. Wang will inform the design of the PA-AKG architecture, particularly focusing on knowledge retrieval and extraction, multi-agent orchestration, and knowledge persistence, and the benchmark evaluation~\cite{srivastava2025beyondbench}. \textbf{Co-PI Steinmetz} is a researcher at Sandia National Labs with expertise in human-machine teaming~\cite{chance2023towards,powell2021coordination}, generative AI~\cite{krofcheck2023overview,layton2025mstd}, and trust in AI systems~\cite{naugle2025trusted,steinmetz2025trust}. Dr. Steinmetz will inform the PA-AKG design and support validation efforts against HPC software development workflows at Sandia, leveraging his expertise in trustworthy AI systems to assess usability and accountability in practical HPC development contexts. \textbf{GRA Cusati} is a Ph.D. student in the Department of Computer Science at Virginia Tech with extensive industry experience implementing complex production software in various roles. Jason will lead the design and development of the PA-AKG infrastructure and the overall data collection and analysis efforts.

\paragraph{Technology Transfer and Industry Partner.}

In either case, add a paragraph to address Criterion 4 (Commercialization Potential).}

\paragraph{Resources.} Virginia Tech's Advanced Research Computing\footnote{\url{https://arc.vt.edu/}} provides GPU cluster infrastructure for large-scale KG construction and LLM inference workloads. The Department of Computer Science is well equipped to support research in the areas described in the proposal. To facilitate wide ranging research activities, major services provided by the department include: a Computer Science Research Virtual Machine cluster (CSRVM) with flexibility to create large scale VMs; ; a secure, modern, climate-controlled server room and workspace to work directly with servers and equipment; and computing resources with cutting-edge high-performance computing and visualization resources. The team will access publicly available scientific software corpora and documentation (\eg GitHub, arXiv, IEEE Xplore) and pre-trained language models (\eg Llama, GPT-4) for extraction and orchestration. Sandia National Laboratories will contribute access to HPC performance benchmarks, legacy scientific codebases, and DOE-relevant software documentation corpora for secondary domain validation, ensuring the research addresses realistic national laboratory software development challenges.
